So let's jump into the first part of the session now. Okay, now immediately after you close the sale, you need to essentially set the stage for a long-term relation. Now remember, closing the sales without getting the five elements of the straight line system to line up for a single moment in time. Essentially building up those three tens, then using the pain threshold, then if necessary the action threshold to get someone to say yes, to get them to buy, right? Now once you close the sale, your job shifts to make sure that the certainty that you've built up continues to build over time so they continue to feel like they made the right decision. Now to that end, there are three things you can do right away to help essentially wow your customers and to create a top-match customer experience that will bind them to you for the long-term. Now let's go through them. Number one, first you want to realize that when you get someone to say yes, that's just the beginning of the sale. It's not the end of the process. See the biggest mistake that most sales people make is that the moment they close the sale, they let their guard down. They drop their level of certainty, their own state starts to sort of become more neutral, right? Essentially they lower their energy level and reality, what you need to do is the exact opposite. In other words, your goal is that when you close the sale, you want to immediately anchor your customer into this feeling that they've made the right decision. You want to give them the certainty that everything is going to continue to work out exactly as they envision it over the long-term. The way you do this is to use the moment when you actually get them to say yes, I want you to trigger yourself into a state of even higher certainty. As you raise your energy level after the customer says yes versus lowering it. That's really essentially the beginning of you taking your state to an even higher level. The second thing is you want to follow up the sale with a personal thank you note. Let me say this, writing thank you notes is a law start. Because of that, handwritten notes make an even bigger impact than they ever have. In other words, people don't really get them anymore, so when they actually get one, they're blown away. Let me give you an example. I was out for lunch the other day with my book agent, Joe Gott, a great guy, good friend. We were talking about a man named Michael Opus, who was a very famous agent in Hollywood at CA. He actually was the founder of CA. He was the first super agent, so to speak. He doesn't have a very good reputation with people. He's sort of a very controversial, hard-edge character. I asked Joe, I said, Joe, I don't understand. Why is it? What was it, essentially, that made Michael Opus such a huge success? That made him essentially the agent to the stars. Joe said, here's what it was, Jordan. This guy was the world's greatest ingratiator. I'm like, ingratiator. He goes, listen, he had a team of four people, assistants, which would sit around all day long and scour through the trades. Anytime he saw it, someone got an award or received any type of reward, he would send them a congratulatory note or a small gift. He'd write these really heartwarming notes as if he really cared in spite of the fact that he had a reputation for having a heart made of stone. But because of these wonderfully ingratiating congratulatory notes, and by sending it out relentlessly again and again and again over time, people began to say, you know, wow, he must really care about me despite the fact he doesn't care about anybody else. And it worked. And he used this system of writing these ingratiating notes as one of the primary recruiting tools on his way to becoming the most powerful Hollywood agent in history. Okay, so don't underestimate the power of literally, like, you know, a three-sentence note to somebody after they've, you know, gotten the product that you sold and whatever it might be that you're selling. It's a heartwarming, emotional few sentences, and the impact it has on the person is staggering. Now to do this effectively, essentially to become an expert ingratiator, to become an expert at sending out thank you notes, you have to, of course, gather massive intelligence. And of course you can, or really you don't want to do it the way that Michael Obertz did it, where you have a team of people going out there and scouring the trade journals and the magazines and every publication, because in reality it's a very expensive proposition for most salespeople who are getting started, right? Even advanced salespeople, in fact, you know, it's a pretty expensive proposition. It's very, very complicated. So for right now, let's focus on using this technique of ingratiation, sending out thank you notes for people that you've already opened up as accounts, people you've just opened up. And let's say you've just opened up a sale and now it's time to use this tactic to build massive trust with the client. Now if the starters, if you've done your job right during the actual sale, then you've stuck to your straight line prospecting and you'll have gathered tons of intelligence, essentially all the information you need to personalize a message to the customer. Now to that end, if you've just closed the sale and you don't feel like you really have all the intelligence you need, what you can do is you go back into a revised qualification process, except this time you focus a bit more on their personal life. And what I call this is identifying someone's aliveness. What makes them feel alive outside of work? In fact, remember during the rapport building module, we spoke about the fish on the wall, right? When you walk into someone's office and then that big fish there, don't try to get into rapport by saying, oh, that's a nice fish, right? Every other salesperson walked in and said, that's a nice fish on the wall. That's not what generates rapport before you've closed the sale. However, after you've closed the sale, it's all about the fish. In other words, it's a shift, a fundamental shift from being strictly business to actually becoming a person who cares about the person's life. So again, that fish on the wall that was taboo before you closed the sale now becomes very pertinent after the sale. So when you're in this revised qualification process, you make this subtle shift of finding a bit more about the person's personal life. And then when you actually write the thank you note or we'll talk about something ethical gifts a bit later, you have something to go by so that can really hit home with them and move them emotionally because the key to writing effective thank you notes is to move someone emotionally. Now to be clear here, I want you to be sure to file this information away for later use at the appropriate time, whether it's a holiday, an anniversary, a contract renewal and so on. But the key is to actually use the information you gather. You write a thank you note to send the thank you gift where appropriate as a follow up to the sale. Now to do this effectively, there needs to be something that's specific to the customer. Here's an example. Let's say you're a financial planner and you just closed the client and he has a daughter who's going to go to college one day. You say, dear John, thank you for entrusting with your business. I look forward to helping you build your daughter Emily's college fund. That's just an example and you go on a little bit after that. But that's an example of how you take a thank you note and personalize it to what matters most essentially his daughter's education. So let me reiterate here. Number one was when you close the sale, you don't drop your state. You actually elevate your state and anchor in that good feeling with your client. Number two was to send that thank you note, that powerful thank you note using some emotional tugs on his heartstrings. And now is number three. The third key here is immediately after you close the sale is to follow up personally to make sure that everything is turning out exactly as planned during the actual sales conversation. In other words, this is about ensuring relationship integrity, taking responsibility to make sure that your customer actually gets exactly what he or she was promised. See the danger zone in most businesses happens at this point where there's a handoff when the sales person turns over a new customer to the product fulfillment department. This is an area where it's so easy to have a huge impact with very little effort. All you need to do is to make sure the product is being fulfilled as promised and to check in with your client when the appropriate time comes. Now what's important here is to not try to upsell your client every single time you talk to them. Just check in with them periodically and this will ingratiate them to you. In other words, don't make it so every time you call it's a sales call. You have what's called schmooze calls. We just call up, ask them how things are going, if everything is okay, you can help them in any way and that call goes a long way to establishing trust and building the long term relationship. Take ownership by the way of their satisfaction. If they're feeling good, you'll take ownership of yourself and if something isn't right, make sure you take ownership of any dissatisfaction they might have with your business. Don't pass the buck. If something isn't right, you follow up directly with the person in your organization who can help fix it. Don't just tell your customer, yeah, I'll call this person and they'll take care of it and let me know if you still have any problems after that. That's not the sort of behavior that's going to create a customer for life. You need to follow up with them yourself to make sure any challenges they might be facing have been taken care of. That's the kind of personal attention that goes a long way towards creating a relationship that's going to continue to grow over time. One final note here, it has to do with the power of language and I touched on this in detail during the looping module and I'm sure you've been through that by now because typically this is one of the last modules that everyone goes through. What I talk about in looping is that certain language patterns will create an unconscious emotional feeling in someone that can either move them in the right direction or of course in the wrong direction. Now, in the case of after the sale, there's one specific language pattern that makes an absolutely huge difference in how your customer perceives you when your customer asks you a question or there's any sort of problem, there's a huge difference to you saying, I need to get back to you or I have to get back to you versus I'd be glad to get back to you. See that language pattern, I'd be glad to get back to you is very, very powerful because it shows the customer that you're not doing it as an obligation but it's something that you actually embrace. And this goes a really long way towards essentially painting the picture in the customer's mind that you're going to hold their hand every step of the way and you'll do it gladly, not grudgingly. So those are the three things that you must do immediately post sale. First, remember keep your state up, right? So you build momentum past the point of the sale when you close. That's number one. Number two, send an effective and specific thoughtful thank you note to your client and put in there some language patterns that will create an emotional response, not like, dear John, thanks for the sale, Jordan. I'm not talking about that, I'm talking about, you know, again, I look forward to working with you in the future. Thank you so much for the confidence. I look forward to helping you build your daughter's college fund. Those are the sort of emotional words that you want to include in there are really going to go a long way towards ingratiating you to the client. And finally, number three, I want you to take responsibility that the products have filled exactly as you promised and your customer remains satisfied long after the sale was made. Again, if anything negative happens to them or any problems arise, which often does happen after you close the sale, make sure you're the one that fouls up with them. Don't just say, here's the customer service number and good luck. You know, call me if you have any problems. You're the guy that sees things through for them and holds their hand every step of the way and you're glad to do it.
